summary: Test assertion failures due to Unicode emoji encoding mismatch on Windows. The test expects the emoji character '<💻>' but receives mojibake '<ðŸ'»>', indicating incorrect UTF-8 decoding or encoding handling.
root_cause: Unicode/UTF-8 encoding mismatch in test assertions comparing emoji characters. The test expects properly decoded emoji but receives mojibake (garbled characters), which is characteristic of UTF-8 bytes being interpreted with the wrong encoding on Windows.

evidence.reason: Direct evidence of test assertion failure; the mojibake pattern (ðŸ'») is the result of UTF-8 bytes being decoded with incorrect encoding, a classic Windows character encoding issue
Shows 4 instances of this AssertionError occurring during test execution

evidence.quote: Job: Windows / Build and test (x64, switch-case)
evidence.reason: Confirms test is running on Windows, where default character encodings can differ from Unix/Linux and may cause emoji/UTF-8 handling issues

suggested_fix: Verify and explicitly set UTF-8 encoding in the test file or test runner. Check if the test is properly handling emoji characters on Windows by ensuring the source file is saved as UTF-8 with BOM or by explicitly setting PYTHONIOENCODING=utf-8 environment variable during test execution.

model_info._backfill_note: Pre-Codex-2026-05-14 F2 fix. The Claude shim now persists _model_info from every API call; this row predates that emit so resolved_model and usage are not recoverable. requested_model is set to the shim-level alias (haiku for v1, sonnet for v2) which is what the post-fix shim emits.
